name: Solutions
subject: Chemistry

sections.heading: Types of Solutions
sections.body:
A solution is a homogeneous mixture of two or more components whose composition and properties are uniform throughout. The component present in the largest amount is the solvent; the rest are solutes. Solutions are classified by the physical state of the solvent — gaseous, liquid, or solid — and each can dissolve gas, liquid, or solid solutes (nine combinations in total).

Examples: air (gas in gas), oxygen in water (gas in liquid), brass (solid in solid). NCERT focuses on binary liquid solutions.

sections.heading: Concentration Units
sections.body:
Concentration can be expressed qualitatively (dilute/concentrated) or quantitatively. The standard quantitative measures are:

  • Mass % (w/w) = (mass of component / total mass) × 100
  • Volume % (v/v) = (volume of component / total volume) × 100
  • Mass by volume % (w/v) — common in medicine.
  • Parts per million (ppm) = (parts of component / total parts) × 10⁶ — used for very dilute solutions.
  • Mole fraction (x) = moles of component / total moles. Sum of mole fractions of all components = 1.
  • Molarity (M) = moles of solute / volume of solution in litres. Temperature dependent because volume changes with T.
  • Molality (m) = moles of solute / mass of solvent in kg. Temperature independent, hence preferred for colligative properties.

sections.heading: Solubility — Solid in Liquid and Gas in Liquid
sections.body:
Solubility is the maximum amount of a solute that dissolves in a given amount of solvent at a specified temperature. Like dissolves like: polar solutes dissolve in polar solvents, non-polar in non-polar.

Effect of temperature on solid solubility follows Le Chatelier's principle. If dissolution is endothermic (ΔHsol > 0), solubility increases with T; if exothermic, it decreases. Pressure has negligible effect on solid–liquid solubility.

Henry's Law for gases: the partial pressure of a gas above a solution is proportional to its mole fraction in the solution: p = KH · x. Higher KH means lower solubility. KH increases with temperature, so gas solubility decreases on heating (why aquatic life suffers in warm water).

Applications: aerated drinks bottled at high pressure; deep-sea divers' tanks use He-diluted air to avoid bends; climbers at altitude suffer anoxia due to lower O2 partial pressure.

sections.heading: Vapour Pressure of Liquid Solutions — Raoult's Law
sections.body:
Raoult's Law for a solution of two volatile liquids: the partial vapour pressure of each component is proportional to its mole fraction in the liquid phase.

p1 = p1° · x1; p2 = p2° · x2. Total pressure: ptotal = p1° + (p2° − p1°)·x2, which is linear in x2.

For a solution containing a non-volatile solute, only the solvent contributes to vapour pressure: psolution = p1° · x1. Relative lowering: (p1° − psolution) / p1° = x2 (mole fraction of solute). This is a colligative property.

Raoult's law is a special case of Henry's law where KH = p°.

sections.heading: Ideal and Non-Ideal Solutions
sections.body:
Ideal solutions obey Raoult's law over the entire concentration range. They have ΔHmix = 0 and ΔVmix = 0 because solute–solvent interactions are identical to solute–solute and solvent–solvent. Examples: n-hexane + n-heptane, benzene + toluene, chlorobenzene + bromobenzene.

Non-ideal solutions deviate from Raoult's law:

  • Positive deviation: A–B interactions weaker than A–A / B–B; vapour pressure higher than predicted; ΔHmix > 0; ΔVmix > 0. e.g. ethanol + acetone, CS2 + acetone.
  • Negative deviation: A–B interactions stronger (often H-bonding); vapour pressure lower than predicted; ΔHmix < 0; ΔVmix < 0. e.g. chloroform + acetone (forms H-bond), HNO3 + H2O.

Azeotropes: binary mixtures with constant boiling point that distill without composition change. Minimum boiling azeotrope arises from large positive deviation (95% ethanol + 5% water). Maximum boiling azeotrope from large negative deviation (68% HNO3 + 32% H2O).

sections.heading: Colligative Properties — Relative Lowering, Boiling Point Elevation, Freezing Point Depression
sections.body:
Colligative properties depend on the number of solute particles, not their identity.

1. Relative lowering of vapour pressure: ΔP/P° = xsolute. Used to determine molar mass: M2 = (w2 · M1 · p1°) / [(p1° − ps) · w1] (dilute approximation).

2. Elevation of boiling point: ΔTb = Kb · m, where Kb is the molal elevation constant (ebullioscopic constant). Solute makes boiling point higher because vapour pressure is lowered.

3. Depression of freezing point: ΔTf = Kf · m, where Kf is the molal depression constant (cryoscopic constant). Used in antifreeze (ethylene glycol in radiators), salt-on-roads.

Kb and Kf depend only on the solvent: Kb = R·M1·Tb² / (1000·ΔHvap) and similarly for Kf with ΔHfus.

sections.heading: Osmotic Pressure
sections.body:
Osmosis: solvent flow through a semipermeable membrane from low to high solute concentration. Osmotic pressure (π) is the extra pressure that must be applied to the solution side to stop osmosis.

Van't Hoff equation: π = C·R·T = (n2/V)·R·T. Hence M2 = w2·R·T / (π·V). This is the most accurate method for determining molar mass of polymers and proteins because π is measurable even at very dilute concentration and at room temperature.

Isotonic: same osmotic pressure (e.g. 0.9% NaCl is isotonic with blood plasma). Hypertonic: higher π — water flows out of cells (plasmolysis). Hypotonic: lower π — water flows in (cells burst). Reverse osmosis: applying pressure greater than π pushes solvent out — used for desalination of sea water using cellulose acetate membranes.

sections.heading: Abnormal Molar Masses and Van't Hoff Factor
sections.body:
If the solute associates or dissociates in solution, the experimentally determined molar mass differs from the true value. Van't Hoff introduced the factor i to correct colligative properties:

i = (observed colligative property) / (calculated colligative property) = (normal molar mass) / (abnormal molar mass) = (number of particles after) / (number of particles before).

  • Dissociation (e.g. NaCl → Na⁺ + Cl⁻): i > 1. KCl, NaCl ideally i = 2; BaCl2 ideally i = 3.
  • Association (e.g. acetic acid dimer in benzene): i < 1.

Modified equations: ΔTb = i·Kb·m; ΔTf = i·Kf·m; π = i·CRT; ΔP/P° = i·x2.

Degree of dissociation α = (i − 1)/(n − 1). Degree of association α = (1 − i) · n/(n − 1).

summary: Solutions are homogeneous mixtures characterised by composition (mole fraction, molarity, molality, ppm). Henry's law (p = K_H·x) governs gas solubility; Raoult's law (p_i = p_i°·x_i) governs vapour pressure of volatile components. Ideal solutions show ΔH_mix = ΔV_mix = 0; non-ideal solutions show positive (weaker A–B interaction) or negative (H-bonding A–B) deviations and may form azeotropes. The four colligative properties — relative lowering of vapour pressure, ΔT_b = K_b·m, ΔT_f = K_f·m, π = CRT — depend only on the number of solute particles and are used to determine molar masses. Dissociation/association introduce the van't Hoff factor i to correct results.
pitfalls: Confusing molarity (per L of solution) with molality (per kg of solvent) — molality is T-independent.
Forgetting to include i for ionic/associated solutes in colligative formulae.
Mixing up positive vs negative deviation: positive deviation = higher vapour pressure = weaker A–B interactions.
Assuming K_b and K_f depend on solute — they depend only on solvent.
Treating azeotropes as separable by simple distillation — they are not.
Using mole fraction of solute when the formula calls for mole fraction of solvent (or vice versa) in vapour pressure calculations.
